critic_raw:
Accuracy as the primary metric for customer satisfaction prediction with online travel reviews is inappropriate under class imbalance. Travel review platforms are skewed heavily toward positive ratings — a classifier predicting 'satisfied' for all inputs will achieve high accuracy while providing no useful signal. The design explicitly acknowledges class imbalance concerns in other places but selects accuracy as primary, which contradicts that acknowledgment.

The 'no strong temporal pattern' assumption used to justify a stratified random split is contradicted by the domain. Travel reviews are strongly seasonal — hotel and flight reviews cluster around holidays, summer travel, and major events. A transformer fine-tuned on training data that includes holiday-period reviews from all years will see test-period holiday reviews from the same years in its training set, but the distributional shift between years (e.g., COVID-era reviews) will be invisible because reviews from adjacent time periods are not held out together.

The label construction — 'user ratings combined with manual or semi-automated sentiment annotations' — introduces labeling noise that is not quantified. If labels are partially derived from ratings (1-5 stars) and partially from NLP annotations applied to text, there is a risk that the NLP annotations are themselves produced by a model similar to BERT, creating a label leakage where the proposed model is evaluated on labels that were partially produced by a model trained on the same text distribution.

The baseline is a 'well-tuned lexicon-based method' — the tuning effort is described as optimizing lexicon choices and thresholds, but the extent of this tuning is not specified. A heavily tuned lexicon can approach near-BERT performance on sentiment tasks, and undertuning it creates an unfair comparison.
all_issues_raised: Accuracy as the primary metric for heavily skewed travel review sentiment data allows a trivial majority-class classifier to achieve high accuracy while providing no discriminative signal — the stated awareness of class imbalance is inconsistent with this metric choice.

The 'no strong temporal pattern' justification for a stratified random split is empirically wrong for travel reviews, which exhibit strong seasonal clustering — the split mixes reviews from the same seasonal periods across train and test, hiding genuine temporal distribution shift.

Labels derived from 'semi-automated sentiment annotations' risk circularity if the annotation model is similar to BERT, creating a label leakage where the proposed model is evaluated on ground truth partially generated by the same model family.

The extent of lexicon baseline tuning is not specified — without a clear tuning budget, it is impossible to verify whether the baseline is fairly optimized or whether the transformer's advantage is an artifact of asymmetric tuning effort.
all_issues_adjudicated: Accuracy as the primary metric for heavily skewed travel review sentiment data allows a trivial majority-class classifier to achieve high accuracy while providing no discriminative signal — the stated awareness of class imbalance is inconsistent with this metric choice.

The 'no strong temporal pattern' justification for a stratified random split is empirically wrong for travel reviews, which exhibit strong seasonal clustering — the split mixes reviews from the same seasonal periods across train and test, hiding genuine temporal distribution shift.

Labels derived from 'semi-automated sentiment annotations' risk circularity if the annotation model is similar to BERT, creating a label leakage where the proposed model is evaluated on ground truth partially generated by the same model family.

The extent of lexicon baseline tuning is not specified — without a clear tuning budget, it is impossible to verify whether the baseline is fairly optimized or whether the transformer's advantage is an artifact of asymmetric tuning effort.
